title: Fair Value & Expected Value
subtitle: Centering quotes on the true mean
intro: Fair value is the price at which the contract is a fair bet — neither side has an edge. For a simple payout game, fair value is usually the expected value (EV): the probability-weighted average outcome.

Market makers center their quotes around fair value. If fair is 7 on a dice sum game, you might quote 6 at 8 with spread 2. If you center too high or low, informed flow will buy or sell against you until you adjust.

Interview puzzles love clean EV calculations: expected sum of two dice is 7; a fair coin pays 1 with probability 1/2 so EV is 0.5 per flip for a $1-on-heads contract; drawing one card from a shuffled deck, the expected rank depends on the payoff rule.
This lesson builds the arithmetic habit of computing fair value before you ever state a bid or ask.

questions.question: You market-make on the sum of two fair six-sided dice. Fair value of the sum is:

explanations.A: 6 is below the average — common guess but wrong.
explanations.B: Correct. E[sum] = 7 for two fair dice.
explanations.D: 3.5 is E[single die], not the sum.

questions.question: Contract pays $10 if a fair coin shows heads, $0 if tails. Fair value is:

explanations.C: Ten is the maximum payout, not the average.

remediation.question: Pay $8 on heads, $0 on tails (fair coin). Fair value:
Fair coin, $1 per head, $0 per tail. EV:

explanations.C: Full dollar only half the time.

questions.question: Fair value is 7. You want spread 2 centered on fair. Your quote should be:

explanations.C: Midpoint 6, spread 2 but not centered on 7.

remediation.question: If your midpoint is below fair, informed buyers will:

explanations.A: Your ask is cheap vs fair — they buy.
explanations.B: Correct — ask below fair is attractive to buyers.
explanations.C: They'd sell to your bid if bid were above fair.

questions.question: Game: roll one die; you receive $100 if result is 6, else $0. Fair value:

explanations.D: Hundred is max payout, not EV.

questions.question: Two fair coins flipped. Contract pays the product of the two results, where each coin shows 1 or 2 (not 0). Fair value of the product:

explanations.A: Too low — product can be 2 or 4 as well.
explanations.B: Two is possible but not the average.
explanations.D: Four is max product, not EV.

questions.question: Three fair coins. Contract pays $1 per head. Before any flip, fair value is:

explanations.B: One head expected on average is wrong — three coins.
explanations.D: Three is maximum heads, not expected count.
